---
title: "30 · Q-Learning with FA, and the Deadly Triad"
subtitle: "Off-policy + bootstrapping + FA can diverge. Baird's counterexample makes this concrete."
lesson-id: "mfrl.q-learning-with-fa"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.td-with-function-approximation"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "drafted"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you read on, can you:

1. State **tabular** [Q-learning](/glossary.qmd#q-learning) — the
   $\max_{a'}$ update rule?
2. State **semi-gradient TD** with linear function approximation
   from [Lesson 29](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd)?
3. Define **on-policy** versus **off-policy** sampling in your own words?

If any answer is no, return to those lessons before continuing.
:::

## Touch

Picture seven states arranged like the spokes of a partial fan: states 1
through 6 each have a single action that always sends you to state 7. State
7 has two actions: a "dashed" action that takes you back to a uniformly
random state in $\{1, \ldots, 6\}$, and a "solid" action that returns you to
state 7. Reward is zero everywhere; $\gamma = 0.99$.

This is **Baird's counterexample**, due to Leemon Baird (1995). The true
value of every state is zero — every reward is zero, so $v_\pi \equiv 0$. We
will use Q-learning with linear function approximation to *estimate* these
values from data sampled by a behaviour policy that always picks the dashed
action.

Now imagine a button labelled **"do one Q-learning step with linear FA"**. You
click it once. Then again. Then again. Watch the magnitudes of the parameter
vector $w$ on a log scale.

::: {.lesson-beat}
The true value is $0$. The features are linear. The data is plentiful.
The update has been used safely a thousand times in the tabular case.
And yet $\|w\|$ blows up to infinity.
:::

## Wonder

Don't write yet — just feel out the puzzle.

1. In the tabular case Q-learning is one of the most well-behaved algorithms
   in RL. What feature of the tabular case made it safe?
2. Q-learning bootstraps (its target uses $\hat q$). It is off-policy (the
   $\max_{a'}$ doesn't follow the behaviour policy). And here we add function
   approximation. What does each ingredient *individually* contribute?
3. If you removed any one of the three, would the divergence stop?

## Discover

Work through these in order.

### Problem 1 — write down Q-learning with FA

For tabular Q-learning the update on $(s, a, r, s')$ is
$q(s, a) \leftarrow q(s, a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s', a') - q(s, a)]$.
Translate this to function approximation with $\hat q(s, a; w)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Re-use the semi-gradient pattern from Lesson 29.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$w \;\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\bigl[r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s', a'; w) - \hat q(s, a; w)\bigr]\,\nabla_w \hat q(s, a; w).$$

The TD error uses $\max_{a'}$ — that's what makes it Q-learning rather than
Sarsa. Same semi-gradient catch as before: the target's $w$-dependence
through both $\hat q(s', \cdot; w)$ and the implicit $\arg\max$ is ignored.
</details>

### Problem 2 — what makes a tabular update safe?

In tabular Q-learning, an update at $(s, a)$ changes only the entry
$q(s, a)$. Why does that matter for stability?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Could a single update damage estimates at *other* state-action pairs?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
No interference. An update at $(s, a)$ leaves every other $q(s', a')$ alone,
so the contraction property of the Bellman optimality operator passes
through cleanly: every entry moves toward its target by a $(1-\alpha)$
fraction. With function approximation, an update at $(s, a)$ moves $w$ —
and that changes $\hat q$ at *every* state-action pair. Updates interfere.
</details>

### Problem 3 — and what makes off-policy worse?

In on-policy methods, the sampling distribution $\mu$ matches what the
policy you're evaluating actually visits. In off-policy methods (Q-learning
included), $\mu$ is whatever the behaviour policy generates, which can be
*very* different from the distribution that the greedy policy would induce.

Why does this mismatch matter when you also have FA?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Recall Lesson 29: the convergence proof depends on the projection being a
contraction in the $\mu$-weighted norm — and $\mu$ has to be the right
distribution.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The Bellman-optimality operator is a contraction in the $\sup$-norm. The
projection onto a linear subspace is a contraction in the
$\mu$-weighted norm. The two norms agree only when $\mu$ matches the
policy. With off-policy data, the composition (projection ∘ Bellman) is no
longer guaranteed to be a contraction in any single norm — and the iteration
can have eigenvalues with magnitude > 1, i.e., it diverges.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Leemon Baird's 1995 paper** "Residual Algorithms" presented a 7-state
linear FA where Q-learning's parameters grow without bound. It was the
slap-in-the-face that forced the RL community to take stability seriously
under function approximation — and motivated the engineering tricks
(replay, target networks) that DQN would adopt 18 years later.
:::

### Problem 4 — name the three ingredients

You have been circling around three properties of the algorithm. Name them.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What do *Q-learning + FA* combine that semi-gradient TD-on-policy with linear
FA does not?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
1. **Bootstrapping** — the target uses $\hat q$ at the next state.
2. **Off-policy** — the data comes from a policy different from the one
   being learnt about (here, the greedy policy implicit in $\max_{a'}$).
3. **Function approximation** — $\hat q$ is parameterised, so updates
   interfere.

These three together are the **deadly triad**. Take any *one* away and
divergence stops being generic; have all three and divergence is on the table.
</details>

### Problem 5 — break the triad

For each of the three ingredients, name an existing algorithm in this track
that already removes it.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You learnt one each in lessons 17, 24, and the tabular chapter of lesson 26.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- Remove **bootstrapping** → Monte Carlo policy evaluation
  ([Lesson 17](../17-mc-basic/index.qmd)). Uses full episode returns;
  no $\hat q$ in the target.
- Remove **off-policy** → on-policy
  [Sarsa](/glossary.qmd#sarsa) ([Lesson 24](../24-sarsa/index.qmd)). Same
  policy generates data and is evaluated.
- Remove **function approximation** → tabular Q-learning
  ([Lesson 26](../26-q-learning/index.qmd)). One entry per $(s, a)$.

Each of these is provably convergent under standard step-size conditions.
The trouble starts only when all three are stacked together.
:::
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
**The deadly triad: bootstrapping + off-policy + FA can diverge.** Each
ingredient on its own is fine. Together, they break the contraction
arguments that guarantee stability in the tabular or on-policy or MC cases.
This is not a sign of a buggy algorithm — it's a real mathematical hazard
that motivates every "trick" in modern deep RL.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Q-learning with function approximation.** For parameterised
$\hat q(s, a; w)$ and a transition $(s, a, r, s')$,

$$w \;\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\bigl[r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat q(s', a'; w) - \hat q(s, a; w)\bigr]\,\nabla_w \hat q(s, a; w).$$

**The deadly triad.** *Bootstrapping*, *off-policy* sampling, and *function
approximation*. Combining all three can cause the iteration above to diverge,
even with linear FA, even on a small finite MDP. Baird's counterexample is
the canonical demonstration.

:::

A mnemonic, in three words:

> *Boot. Off. Approx. — pick at most two safely.*

## Apply

### Worked example — sketch of Baird's divergence

Baird's MDP has 7 states and a single action that, regardless of which
state, sends you uniformly into states 1–6. State 7 is special. Linear FA
uses 8 features per state, chosen so that one feature in particular acts as
a "bias" present in every state. The behaviour policy always selects the
non-greedy ("dashed") action.

The crucial fact: with this feature design, the linear update matrix
implicit in semi-gradient Q-learning has an eigenvalue greater than 1 in
magnitude. Iterating the update therefore *amplifies* certain components of
$w$ exponentially. After a few hundred steps the parameters reach
$10^{20}$ and beyond, even though the true value is identically zero.

You don't need the algebra. You need the lesson: this is not a bug, this is
not pathological floating-point — it's the analytic behaviour of
(Bellman-optimality ∘ projection) when $\mu$ doesn't match $\pi$ and the
projection isn't a contraction in the relevant norm.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Does tabular Q-learning suffer the deadly triad? Justify.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Walk through the three ingredients.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Tabular Q-learning is off-policy (the target uses $\max_{a'}$, not the
behaviour policy) and bootstraps. It is **not** function approximation —
each state-action pair has its own entry. So only two of the three
ingredients are present, and tabular Q-learning is provably convergent.
</details>

**W2.** Is on-policy semi-gradient TD with linear FA in the deadly triad?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Which of the three ingredients are present?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Bootstrapping ✓; FA ✓; off-policy ✗. So only two ingredients — and the
Tsitsiklis–Van Roy theorem (Lesson 29) guarantees convergence.
</details>

**W3.** Q-learning with FA — even *linear* FA — can diverge. True or false?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**True.** Baird's counterexample uses linear FA. Non-linearity isn't the
culprit; off-policy + bootstrapping + interference is enough.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A practitioner says: "I'll just clip $\|w\|$ to bound it." Does that
solve Baird's problem?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Clipping prevents overflow, but does the *direction* converge to anything
sensible?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
No. Clipping prevents numerical overflow but the iteration still doesn't
converge to the correct fixed point — it bounces around inside the clip
ball. The true values are zero; clipped Q-learning still produces wrong
predictions and inconsistent policy improvement. Clipping is a symptom
treatment, not a cure.
</details>

**P2.** Which of the three deadly-triad ingredients does **DQN** eliminate?
Spoiler hint allowed: none — DQN keeps all three. Then how does DQN cope?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
DQN is the next lesson. Skim its title.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
None of the three. DQN is bootstrapping, off-policy, *and* uses neural
network FA — every triad ingredient is present and amplified. DQN's trick
is **engineering**: a slowly-updated *target network* that decouples the
target from the current $w$ (so it doesn't shift under us during a batch),
and a *replay buffer* that decorrelates samples. These don't break the
triad — they tame it. See [Lesson 31](../31-deep-q-network/index.qmd).
</details>

**P3.** Why does the deadly triad strike at **off-policy** specifically and
not, e.g., on-policy actor-critic with FA?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What happens to the projection norm when $\mu$ matches the policy?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
On-policy means $\mu$ is the stationary distribution of the policy whose
value you're estimating. The projection is then a non-expansion in the
$\mu$-weighted norm, and the composition of (projection ∘ Bellman) inherits
contraction. Off-policy breaks the matching: the projection lives in one
norm, the Bellman operator contracts in another. The composition can have
eigenvalues > 1.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Construct a *minimal* version of Baird's argument: name the smallest
number of states for which the deadly triad can produce divergence with
linear FA, and explain in one sentence why fewer states wouldn't work.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You need enough states for the projection's distortion to manifest *and* for
the off-policy mismatch to do work.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Baird's 7-state example is famously minimal among the well-known
constructions. The essential ingredient is the asymmetry between the
behaviour distribution (which avoids state 7) and the greedy policy (which
prefers state 7). With 2 or 3 states there isn't enough room for the
features to interfere in the required way. (Counterexamples with fewer
states exist in the literature; the *teaching* example most often cited is
the 7-state one.)
</details>

**S2.** Read the update rule for Q-learning with FA aloud. Identify the
location in that one-line formula where **off-policy** enters.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at the bracketed bootstrap target.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The $\max_{a'} \hat q(s', a'; w)$ — that maximum is the *greedy* policy's
value, but the data $(s, a, r, s')$ may have been sampled under any
behaviour policy. The off-policy mismatch is baked into this single
operator: target uses one policy, data comes from another.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Divergence means the algorithm has bugs."** No — Baird's example is
   pure mathematics. The iteration's matrix has eigenvalues > 1.
2. **"Linear FA is always safe."** Linear FA is safe under on-policy
   semi-gradient TD (Lesson 29). With off-policy + bootstrapping it isn't.
3. **"DQN solved the deadly triad."** DQN *tames* it with engineering
   stabilisers; it doesn't *resolve* it theoretically. There is still no
   convergence guarantee for DQN. People run it because it works in
   practice on most tasks they care about — and sometimes it doesn't.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **Q-learning with FA = semi-gradient TD with the bootstrap maximum.**
   The structure of the update is what you'd expect from Lesson 29 with
   $\max_{a'}$ pasted in.
2. **The deadly triad is bootstrapping + off-policy + FA.** Any two are
   safe. All three can diverge. This is mathematics, not folklore.
3. **Baird's 7-state counterexample is the textbook proof.** It is the
   reason DQN exists in its current form.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you name the three ingredients of the deadly triad and one
   algorithm that drops each?
2. Did you, in *Discover*, work out the role of off-policy mismatch *before*
   reading the *Name* beat?
3. Could you sketch why removing FA (i.e., going tabular) makes the same
   off-policy bootstrapped update safe?

- **3 yes:** continue to [Lesson 31 — DQN](../31-deep-q-network/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo P3 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover*, especially Problem 3.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.td-with-function-approximation](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd)** —
  semi-gradient TD with linear FA and the convergence guarantee that
  Q-learning with FA *loses*.

### What this unlocks

- **[DQN](../31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)** — the engineering attack on
  the deadly triad.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Every deep value-based agent (DQN, Double DQN, Rainbow,
distributional Q-learning, IQN) lives in the deadly triad. Their
collective track record of *occasionally* diverging or saturating in
real workloads — Atari games where DQN never escapes the first room,
robotics where Q-values blow up after a buffer flush — is the deadly
triad biting. Engineers add stabilisers; theorists try to break the
triad properly. Both are still active research directions in 2026.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this
track expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §8 (Q-learning with FA, deadly triad). Cite freely;
  never transcribe Zhao's prose. Baird's counterexample originates in
  Baird (1995), "Residual Algorithms".
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school (Discover walks the learner into
  the divergence puzzle problem-first); 3Blue1Brown (the eigenvalue-> 1
  framing for *why* divergence is mathematical, not numerical).
:::
